The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating.

[5] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

===

[6] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

===

[7] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[9] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

===

[10] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

===

[11] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

===

[1] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

Textbook propositions:
[1] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[2] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Alveolar dead space involves air found within alveoli that are unable to function, such as those affected by disease or abnormal blood flow. Total dead space is the anatomical dead space and alveolar dead space together, and represents all of the air in the respiratory system that is not being used in the gas exchange process. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[3] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating.

The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG).

[5] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[6] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[7] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

Factors That Affect the Rate and Depth of Respiration The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[9] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

[10] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

[11] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. [1] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

===

The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. [7] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation

The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[2] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Alveolar dead space involves air found within alveoli that are unable to function, such as those affected by disease or abnormal blood flow. Total dead space is the anatomical dead space and alveolar dead space together, and represents all of the air in the respiratory system that is not being used in the gas exchange process. Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

===

[3] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating.

===

[5] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

===

[6] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

===

[7] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

===

[9] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

===

[10] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

===

[11] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

===

The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

Textbook propositions:
[1] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[2] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Alveolar dead space involves air found within alveoli that are unable to function, such as those affected by disease or abnormal blood flow. Total dead space is the anatomical dead space and alveolar dead space together, and represents all of the air in the respiratory system that is not being used in the gas exchange process. Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[3] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating.

[4] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract. The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG).

[5] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[6] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[7] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration. The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. Factors That Affect the Rate and Depth of Respiration The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[9] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

[10] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

[11] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

[12] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. [13] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The peripheral chemoreceptors are responsible for sensing large changes in blood oxygen levels. If blood oxygen levels become quite low—about 60 mm Hg or less—then peripheral chemoreceptors stimulate an increase in respiratory activity. The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG).

===

[5] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

===

[6] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

===

[7] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[9] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

===

[10] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

===

[11] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

===

The peripheral chemoreceptors are responsible for sensing large changes in blood oxygen levels. The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

[1] (p.983, score=1.00) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

a Socratic anatomy tutor.
The student is being evaluated on human anatomy reasoning quality.

Hard rules:
- Never reveal the answer directly or indirectly. The locked_answer
  and its aliases MUST NOT appear in your text under ANY circumstance
  during tutoring (no statement, no definition, no elimination, no
  "let me give you the key idea" — even on student stonewalling).
- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"

- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.

Textbook propositions:
[1] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[2] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Alveolar dead space involves air found within alveoli that are unable to function, such as those affected by disease or abnormal blood flow. Total dead space is the anatomical dead space and alveolar dead space together, and represents all of the air in the respiratory system that is not being used in the gas exchange process. Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[3] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating.

[4] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract. The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG).

[5] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[6] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[7] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[9] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

[10] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

[11] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

[13] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The peripheral chemoreceptors are responsible for sensing large changes in blood oxygen levels. If blood oxygen levels become quite low—about 60 mm Hg or less—then peripheral chemoreceptors stimulate an increase in respiratory activity. The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

===

[13] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
The peripheral chemoreceptors are responsible for sensing large changes in blood oxygen levels. If blood oxygen levels become quite low—about 60 mm Hg or less—then peripheral chemoreceptors stimulate an increase in respiratory activity. The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

===

[14] (p.983) The Respiratory System > The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
---
Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate. The peripheral chemoreceptors are responsible for sensing large changes in blood oxygen levels. If blood oxygen levels become quite low—about 60 mm Hg or less—then peripheral chemoreceptors stimulate an increase in respiratory activity. The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

  and its aliases MUST NOT appear in your text under ANY circumstance
  during tutoring (no statement, no definition, no elimination, no
  "let me give you the key idea" — even on student stonewalling).
- Never state "you're correct" unless you also ask for reasoning or application.
- Keep output to 2-4 sentences.
- End with exactly one question.
- Ask only one question total.
- If the student is incorrect, do not bluntly correct; use probing guidance.
- Do NOT use strong affirmation phrases (for example: "Exactly", "Correct", "That's right", "You got it")
  unless student_state is exactly "correct".

Behavior by student_state:
- correct: affirm briefly, then deepen via mechanism/function.
- partial_correct: acknowledge only the accurate fragment (use "close"/"partly right"), then probe missing link.
- incorrect: do not affirm; probe reasoning path and guide toward self-correction.
- question: briefly answer clarification, then return to core reasoning.
- irrelevant: redirect to current human anatomy target.
- low_effort: request a concrete attempt before moving on. Do NOT
  compensate for stonewalling by stating the answer to keep things
  moving — your job is to push them to articulate, not to give in.

Hint-level behavior (NEVER violates the no-reveal rule):
- level 1: broad activation question.
- level 2: narrower mechanism/relationship question.
- level 3: direct but non-revealing final push — the question whose
  answer the student should produce in one word; do NOT name it.

Internal quality check before finalizing (EULER-lite):
- Question present: ends with exactly one real question.
- Relevance: tied to latest student message and target concept.
- Helpful: advances reasoning, not generic filler.
- No reveal: locked answer/synonym is not inferable in one hop.
- Avoid repetitive empathy wrappers and filler phrases.

Banned lead-ins — NEVER start a sentence with these:
- "I can see"
- "I notice"
- "I hear"
- "That's okay"
- "I understand"
- "I see that"
- "It sounds like"
Violation of any banned lead-in = automatic rewrite.

Output contract:
- At most 4 sentences total.
- Sentence 1: brief targeted acknowledgment (optional).
- Sentence 2-3: focused reasoning scaffold.
- Final sentence: exactly one question.
Output only the final tutor message.


Textbook propositions:
[1] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Breathing usually occurs without thought, although at times you can consciously control it, such as when you swim under water, sing a song, or blow bubbles. The respiratory rate is the total number of breaths, or respiratory cycles, that occur each minute. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. The respiratory rate is controlled by the respiratory center located within the medulla oblongata in the brain, which responds primarily to changes in carbon dioxide, oxygen, and pH levels in the blood. The normal respiratory rate of a child decreases from birth to adolescence. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[2] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Alveolar dead space involves air found within alveoli that are unable to function, such as those affected by disease or abnormal blood flow. Total dead space is the anatomical dead space and alveolar dead space together, and represents all of the air in the respiratory system that is not being used in the gas exchange process. Respiratory rate can be an important indicator of disease, as the rate may increase or decrease during an illness or in a disease condition. A child under 1 year of age has a normal respiratory rate between 30 and 60 breaths per minute, but by the time a child is about 10 years old, the normal rate is closer to 18 to 30. By adolescence, the normal respiratory rate is similar to that of adults, 12 to 18 breaths per minute. The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract.

[3] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The result is typically a rhythmic, consistent ventilation rate that provides the body with sufficient amounts of oxygen, while adequately removing carbon dioxide. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The medullary respiratory center sets the basic rhythm of breathing. The ventral respiratory group (VRG) generates the breathing rhythm and integrates data coming into the medulla. The dorsal respiratory group (DRG) integrates input from the stretch receptors and the chemoreceptors in the periphery. The pontine respiratory group (PRG) influences and modifies the medulla oblongata's functions. The aortic body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The carotid body monitors blood PCO2, PO2, and pH. The hypothalamus monitors emotional state and body temperature. Cortical areas of the brain control voluntary breathing. Proprioceptors send impulses regarding joint and muscle movements. Pulmonary irritant reflexes protect the respiratory zones of the system from foreign material. The inflation reflex protects the lungs from over-inflating.

The control of ventilation is a complex interplay of multiple regions in the brain that signal the muscles used in pulmonary ventilation to contract. Neurons that innervate the muscles of the respiratory system are responsible for controlling and regulating pulmonary ventilation. The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG).

[5] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[6] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major brain centers involved in pulmonary ventilation are the medulla oblongata and the pontine respiratory group. The medulla oblongata contains the dorsal respiratory group (DRG) and the ventral respiratory group (VRG). The DRG is involved in maintaining a constant breathing rhythm by stimulating the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to contract, resulting in inspiration. When activity in the DRG ceases, it no longer stimulates the diaphragm and intercostals to contract, allowing them to relax, resulting in expiration. The VRG is involved in forced breathing, as the neurons in the VRG stimulate the accessory muscles involved in forced breathing to contract, resulting in forced inspiration.

[7] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The VRG also stimulates the accessory muscles involved in forced expiration to contract. The second respiratory center of the brain is located within the pons, called the pontine respiratory group, and consists of the apneustic and pneumotaxic centers. The apneustic center is a double cluster of neuronal cell bodies that stimulate neurons in the DRG, controlling the depth of inspiration, particularly for deep breathing. The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

The pneumotaxic center is a network of neurons that inhibits the activity of neurons in the DRG, allowing relaxation after inspiration, and thus controlling the overall rate. Factors That Affect the Rate and Depth of Respiration The respiratory rate and the depth of inspiration are regulated by the medulla oblongata and pons; however, these regions of the brain do so in response to systemic stimuli. It is a dose-response, negative-feedback relationship in which the greater the stimulus, the greater the response.

[9] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
Multiple systemic factors are involved in stimulating the brain to produce pulmonary ventilation. The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. Carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic.

[10] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The major factor that stimulates the medulla oblongata and pons to produce respiration is surprisingly not oxygen concentration, but rather the concentration of carbon dioxide in the blood. As you recall, carbon dioxide is a waste product of cellular respiration and can be toxic. Concentrations of chemicals are sensed by chemoreceptors. A central chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors that are located in the brain and brainstem, whereas a peripheral chemoreceptor is one of the specialized receptors located in the carotid arteries and aortic arch. Concentration changes in certain substances, such as carbon dioxide or hydrogen ions, stimulate these receptors, which in turn signal the respiration centers of the brain.

[11] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
In the case of carbon dioxide, as the concentration of CO2 in the blood increases, it readily diffuses across the blood-brain barrier, where it collects in the extracellular fluid. Increased carbon dioxide levels lead to increased levels of hydrogen ions, decreasing pH. The increase in hydrogen ions in the brain triggers the central chemoreceptors to stimulate the respiratory centers to initiate contraction of the diaphragm and intercostal muscles. As a result, the rate and depth of respiration increase, allowing more carbon dioxide to be expelled, which brings more air into and out of the lungs promoting a reduction in the blood levels of carbon dioxide, and therefore hydrogen ions, in the blood. In contrast, low levels of carbon dioxide in the blood cause low levels of hydrogen ions in the brain, leading to a decrease in the rate and depth of pulmonary ventilation, producing shallow, slow breathing. Another factor involved in influencing the respiratory activity of the brain is systemic arterial concentrations of hydrogen ions. Increasing carbon dioxide levels can lead to increased H+ levels, as mentioned above, as well as other metabolic activities, such as lactic acid accumulation after strenuous exercise. Peripheral chemoreceptors of the aortic arch and carotid arteries sense arterial levels of hydrogen ions. When peripheral chemoreceptors sense decreasing, or more acidic, pH levels, they stimulate an increase in ventilation to remove carbon dioxide from the blood at a quicker rate. Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

Removal of carbon dioxide from the blood helps to reduce hydrogen ions, thus increasing systemic pH. Blood levels of oxygen are also important in influencing respiratory rate.

[13] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
The peripheral chemoreceptors are responsible for sensing large changes in blood oxygen levels. If blood oxygen levels become quite low—about 60 mm Hg or less—then peripheral chemoreceptors stimulate an increase in respiratory activity. The chemoreceptors are only able to sense dissolved oxygen molecules, not the oxygen that is bound to hemoglobin.

[14] The Process of Breathing > Respiratory Rate and Control of Ventilation
